title: Weather system

infobox.value: is a peak or lull in the [sea level pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure#Mean_sea-level_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") distribution, a feature of [synoptic-scale weather](/wiki/Synoptic-scale_weather "Synoptic-scale weather"). The surface pressure at sea level varies minimally, with the lowest value measured 87 kilopascals (26 inHg) and the highest recorded 108.57 kilopascals (32.06 inHg). High- and low-pressure systems evolve due to interactions of temperature differentials in the atmosphere, temperature differences between the atmosphere and water within oceans and lakes, the influence of upper-level disturbances, as well as the amount of solar heating or radiationized cooling an area receives. Pressure systems cause [weather](/wiki/Weather "Weather") to be experienced locally. Low-pressure systems are associated with [clouds](/wiki/Cloud "Cloud") and [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") that minimize temperature changes throughout the day, whereas high-pressure systems normally associate with dry weather and mostly clear skies with larger diurnal temperature changes due to greater radiation at night and greater sunshine during the day. Pressure systems are analyzed by those in the field of [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology") within [surface weather maps](/wiki/Surface_weather_analysis "Surface weather analysis").

[![This is a good article. Click here for more information.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/94/Symbol_support_vote.svg/20px-Symbol_support_vote.svg.png)](/wiki/Wikipedia:Good_articles* "This is a good article. Click here for more information.")

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Weather system](/w/index.php?title=Weather_system&redirect=no "Weather system"))

Relative peak or lull in the sea level pressure distribution

For pressure systems and safety, see [Pressure vessel](/wiki/Pressure_vessel "Pressure vessel").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Surface_analysis.gif/250px-Surface_analysis.gif)](/wiki/File:Surface_analysis.gif)

Map of pressure systems across North America

A **pressure system** is a peak or lull in the [sea level pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure#Mean_sea-level_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") distribution, a feature of [synoptic-scale weather](/wiki/Synoptic-scale_weather "Synoptic-scale weather"). The surface pressure at sea level varies minimally, with the lowest value measured 87 kilopascals (26 inHg) and the highest recorded 108.57 kilopascals (32.06 inHg). High- and low-pressure systems evolve due to interactions of temperature differentials in the atmosphere, temperature differences between the atmosphere and water within oceans and lakes, the influence of upper-level disturbances, as well as the amount of solar heating or radiationized cooling an area receives. Pressure systems cause [weather](/wiki/Weather "Weather") to be experienced locally. Low-pressure systems are associated with [clouds](/wiki/Cloud "Cloud") and [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") that minimize temperature changes throughout the day, whereas high-pressure systems normally associate with dry weather and mostly clear skies with larger diurnal temperature changes due to greater radiation at night and greater sunshine during the day. Pressure systems are analyzed by those in the field of [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology") within [surface weather maps](/wiki/Surface_weather_analysis "Surface weather analysis").

## Low-pressure system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pressure_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Low-pressure system")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Low_pressure_system_over_Iceland.jpg/250px-Low_pressure_system_over_Iceland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Low_pressure_system_over_Iceland.jpg)

An [extratropical cyclone](/wiki/Extratropical_cyclone "Extratropical cyclone") swirls off the southwestern coast of Iceland.

Main articles: [Low-pressure area](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area") and [Cyclone](/wiki/Cyclone "Cyclone")

A low-pressure area is a region where the [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") at [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") is below that of surrounding locations. Low-pressure systems form under areas of [wind](/wiki/Wind "Wind") divergence that occur in the upper levels of the [troposphere](/wiki/Troposphere "Troposphere").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The formation process of a low-pressure area is known as [cyclogenesis](/wiki/Cyclogenesis "Cyclogenesis").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Within the field of [atmospheric dynamics](/wiki/Meteorology#Dynamic_meteorology "Meteorology"), areas of wind divergence aloft occur in two areas:

* On the east side of upper troughs, which form half of a [Rossby wave](/wiki/Rossby_wave "Rossby wave") within the [Westerlies](/wiki/Westerlies "Westerlies") (a trough with large [wavelength](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength"), which extends through the troposphere).
* Ahead of [embedded shortwave troughs](/wiki/Shortwave_(meteorology) "Shortwave (meteorology)"), which have smaller wavelengths.

Diverging winds aloft ahead of these troughs cause [atmospheric](/wiki/Atmospheric "Atmospheric") lift within the troposphere below, which [lowers surface pressures](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure") as upward motion partially counteracts the force of gravity.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Thermal lows](/wiki/Thermal_low "Thermal low") form due to localized heating caused by greater sunshine over deserts and other land masses. Since localized areas of warm air are less dense than their surroundings, this warmer air rises, which lowers atmospheric pressure near that portion of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s surface.[[4]](#cite_note-thermal-4) Large-scale thermal lows over [continents](/wiki/Continent "Continent") help create pressure gradients that drive [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") circulations.[[5]](#cite_note-cause-5) Low-pressure areas can also form due to organized thunderstorm activity over warm water.[[6]](#cite_note-AOML_FAQ_A7-6) When this occurs over the tropics in concert with the [Intertropical Convergence Zone](/wiki/Intertropical_Convergence_Zone "Intertropical Convergence Zone"), it is known as a [monsoon trough](/wiki/Monsoon_trough "Monsoon trough").[[7]](#cite_note-ams-7) Monsoon troughs reach their northerly extent in August and their southerly extent in February.[[8]](#cite_note-NCFMRF-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-NAVY2-10) When a convective low acquires a well-defined circulation in the tropics it is termed a [tropical cyclone](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone").[[6]](#cite_note-AOML_FAQ_A7-6) Tropical cyclones can form during any month of the year globally, but can occur in either the northern hemisphere or the southern hemisphere during November.[[11]](#cite_note-AOML_FAQ_G1-11)

Atmospheric lift caused by low-level wind convergence into the surface low brings clouds and potentially [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)").[[12]](#cite_note-convection-12) The low-pressure area's cloudy skies act to minimize [diurnal temperature variation](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation"). Since clouds reflect [sunlight](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight"), incoming [shortwave](/wiki/Shortwave_radiation "Shortwave radiation") [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") is less, which causes lower [temperatures](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature") during the day. At night, the absorptive effect of clouds on [outgoing longwave radiation](/wiki/Outgoing_longwave_radiation "Outgoing longwave radiation"), such as heat energy from the surface, allows for warmer diurnal low temperatures in all seasons. The stronger the area of low pressure, the stronger the [winds](/wiki/Wind "Wind") experienced in its vicinity.[[13]](#cite_note-jet-13) Around the world, low-pressure systems are most frequently located over the [Tibetan Plateau](/wiki/Tibetan_Plateau "Tibetan Plateau") and in the lee of the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains").[[14]](#cite_note-extra-14) In Europe—in particular, the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")—recurring low-pressure weather systems are typically known as depressions. The lowest recorded non-tornadic barometric pressure was 870 hectopascals (26 inHg), occurring in the Western Pacific during [Typhoon Tip](/wiki/Typhoon_Tip "Typhoon Tip") on 12 October 1979.[[15]](#cite_note-FAQ1-15)

## High-pressure system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pressure_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: High-pressure system")]

Main articles: [High-pressure area](/wiki/High-pressure_area "High-pressure area") and [Anticyclone](/wiki/Anticyclone "Anticyclone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/High_Pressure.jpg/250px-High_Pressure.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Pressure.jpg)

Satellite image of a high-pressure area south of Australia, evidenced by the clearing in the clouds[[16]](#cite_note-16)

High-pressure systems are frequently associated with light winds at the surface and [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence_(atmosphere) "Subsidence (atmosphere)") through the lower portion of the [troposphere](/wiki/Troposphere "Troposphere"). In general, subsidence will dry out an air mass by [adiabatic](/wiki/Adiabatic_process "Adiabatic process") or compressional heating.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Thus, high pressure typically brings clear skies.[[18]](#cite_note-18) During the day, since no clouds are present to reflect sunlight, there is more incoming shortwave [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") and temperatures rise. At night, the absence of clouds means that [outgoing longwave radiation](/wiki/Outgoing_longwave_radiation "Outgoing longwave radiation") (i.e. heat energy from the surface) is not absorbed, giving cooler [diurnal](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation") low temperatures in all seasons. When surface winds become light, the subsidence produced directly under a high-pressure system can lead to a buildup of particulates in urban areas under the ridge, leading to widespread [haze](/wiki/Haze "Haze").[[19]](#cite_note-19) If the low-level [relative humidity](/wiki/Relative_humidity "Relative humidity") rises towards 100 percent overnight, [fog](/wiki/Fog "Fog") can form.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Strong but vertically shallow high-pressure systems moving from higher latitudes to lower latitudes in the northern hemisphere are associated with continental arctic air masses.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The low, sharp [temperature inversion](/wiki/Inversion_(meteorology) "Inversion (meteorology)") can lead to areas of persistent [stratocumulus](/wiki/Stratocumulus "Stratocumulus") or [stratus cloud](/wiki/Stratus_cloud "Stratus cloud"), known in colloquial terms as anticyclonic gloom. The type of weather brought about by an anticyclone depends on its origin. For example, extensions of the Azores high bubble pressure may bring about anticyclonic gloom during the winter, as they are warmed at the base and will trap moisture as they move over the warmer oceans. High pressure systems that build to the north and extend southwards will often bring clear weather. This is due to being cooled at the base (as opposed to warmed), which helps prevent clouds from forming. The highest barometric pressure ever recorded on Earth was 1,085.7 hectopascals (32.06 inHg) measured in [Tonsontsengel, Mongolia](/wiki/Tosontsengel,_Zavkhan "Tosontsengel, Zavkhan") on 19 December 2001.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Surface weather maps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pressure_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Surface weather maps")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Streamlineanalysispacific.gif/250px-Streamlineanalysispacific.gif)](/wiki/File:Streamlineanalysispacific.gif)

Streamline analysis of the tropical Pacific Ocean

See also: [Surface weather analysis](/wiki/Surface_weather_analysis "Surface weather analysis")

A surface weather analysis is a type of [weather map](/wiki/Weather_map "Weather map") that depicts positions for [high](/wiki/High-pressure_area "High-pressure area")- and [low-pressure areas](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area"), as well as various types of [synoptic scale](/wiki/Synoptic_scale "Synoptic scale") systems such as [frontal zones](/wiki/Weather_fronts "Weather fronts"). Isotherms can be drawn on these maps, which are lines of equal temperature. Isotherms are drawn normally as solid lines at a preferred temperature interval.[[23]](#cite_note-DataAir-23) They show temperature gradients, which can be useful in finding fronts, which are on the warm side of large temperature gradients. By plotting the freezing line, isotherms can be useful in determination of precipitation type. [Mesoscale convective systems](/wiki/Mesoscale_convective_system "Mesoscale convective system") such as [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone"), outflow boundaries and [squall lines](/wiki/Squall_line "Squall line") also are analyzed on surface weather analyses.

Isobaric analysis is performed on these maps, which involves the construction of lines of equal mean [sea level pressure](/wiki/Sea_level_pressure "Sea level pressure"). The innermost closed lines indicate the positions of relative maxima and minima in the pressure field. The minima are called low-pressure areas, and the maxima are called [high-pressure areas](/wiki/High-pressure_area "High-pressure area"). A *High* is often shown as **H**, and a *[low](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area")* is shown as **L**. Elongated areas of low pressure, or troughs, are sometimes plotted as thick, brown dashed lines down the trough axis.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Isobars are commonly used to place surface boundaries from the [horse latitudes](/wiki/Horse_latitudes "Horse latitudes") poleward, while streamline analyses are used in the tropics.[[25]](#cite_note-25) A streamline analysis is a series of arrows oriented parallel to wind, showing wind motion within a certain geographic area. Cs depict cyclonic flow or likely areas of low pressure, while As depict anticyclonic flow or likely positions of high-pressure areas.[[26]](#cite_note-26) An area of confluent streamlines shows the location of [shearlines](/wiki/Weather_front#Stationary_front_and_shearline "Weather front") within the tropics and subtropics.[[27]](#cite_note-DR-27)
